channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Accountability Tracing in Multi-Agent Systems
item.description:
When an LLM agent makes an error, who is accountable? Not the agent—the agent has no legal standing, no authority, no capacity to bear accountability. Accountability always attaches to a human officer. The question is which one.

The answer requires understanding the difference between responsibility and accountability, tracing the delegation chain from the error back to the person who installed the system and issued the grant.

Responsibility vs. Accountability

These are not synonymous, and the confusion between them is a major source of governance failures in multi-agent systems.

item.title: AI Agents in Industrial Control — Overview
item.description:
As of 2026, AI agents are no longer experimental in industrial control systems. They autonomously manage production lines, power generation, water treatment, and network infrastructure at scale—with documented returns exceeding 250% within 24 months. This marks a transition from automation (following rules) to autonomy (reasoning about changing conditions and adapting).

What Has Changed

The 2015-2020 era: AI in industry meant predictive maintenance. A model predicts “bearing will fail in 7 days,” an engineer reads the alert, schedules repairs.

item.title: Industrial AI Agent Architecture Patterns
item.description:
The defining challenge of AI agents in industrial control is not reasoning, but timing. A cloud-hosted neural network can reason about what to do; a safety-critical manufacturing system must do it in 10 milliseconds. The architecture that solves this problem is clear: separate concerns across three layers, each optimized for its constraint.

The Three-Layer Pattern

Layer 1: Deterministic Control (PLC / Safety System)

The PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) or Safety Instrumented System (SIS) remains the safest tool for time-critical, safety-critical decisions. It runs deterministic control loops at microsecond granularity, certified to functional safety standards like IEC 61508.

item.title: OpenClaw Continuous Learning and Behavioral Evolution
item.description:
Overview

OpenClaw achieves continuous learning and behavioral evolution through a three-layer architecture that persists knowledge across sessions, enables agents to autonomously extend capabilities, and filters learning opportunities through managed input gating. Unlike stateless chatbots that reset after each conversation, OpenClaw agents accumulate durable context, self-modify through skill creation, and evolve their decision boundaries through policy mechanisms.

Memory Persistence Layer

OpenClaw treats memory as the primary mechanism for accumulation across sessions. The memory system is Markdown-based and file-first, making it auditable and portable.

item.title: Orchestration vs. Choreography in Multi-Agent Systems
item.description:
The Coordination Problem

When multiple agents must work together, they face a fundamental problem: how do they decide who does what, in what order, and with what information? Two distinct traditions have emerged to solve this problem, each with different assumptions about where authority and information flow.

Orchestration places coordination decision-making in a single point: a central coordinator that knows about all participants, directs their actions, receives their results, and decides what happens next. The coordinator is the locus of control. In Mintzberg’s terms, this is direct supervision—one authority directing the work of others.

item.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration in Practice
item.description:
This text maps between the theoretical concepts in this repository’s library — delegation, role, office, dispatch — and the concrete multi-agent patterns implemented in Claude Code harnesses and the Everything Claude Code (ECC) reference system.

The orchestration/choreography distinction

The W3C Web Services Choreography Working Group (2005) and Milner’s π-calculus (1999) establish a foundational distinction: orchestration is coordination by a central process that directs participants, while choreography is coordination through a shared protocol that each participant follows independently.

item.title: How to Write an Effective Skill
item.description:
A skill is a markdown file that loads into an agent’s context when the agent decides it is relevant. The file has YAML frontmatter for metadata and a markdown body for instructions. The agent reads the frontmatter to decide whether to load the skill. If it loads, the agent follows the body’s instructions. If it does not load, the skill costs nothing — it is inert.

This guide covers how to write skills that activate at the right time and provide useful guidance when they do.

item.title: Agent Instruction File Standards (March 2026)
item.description:
This text surveys the conventions for instructing AI agents via files placed in project repositories. As of March 2026, three conventions have meaningful adoption, several tool-specific alternatives exist, and a governance structure is forming through the Linux Foundation’s Agentic AI Foundation.

1. The core problem

An AI coding agent operating on a codebase needs project-specific context: how to build, how to test, what style conventions to follow, what files to leave alone, what the architecture looks like. An agent without this context hallucinates conventions or applies generic defaults that conflict with project norms.

item.title: Agent Reliability in Production (March 2026)
item.description:
This text synthesizes what the field has learned about making agentic systems reliable as of March 2026. The primary sources are Anthropic’s “Building Effective Agents” (December 2024), Anthropic’s engineering blog on their production multi-agent research system (2025), the MASFT multi-agent failure study (arXiv 2503.13657, March 2025), the Letta agent memory benchmarks (2025), and the enterprise agent evaluation framework (arXiv 2511.14136, November 2025).

1. Start with the simplest thing that works

Anthropic’s central recommendation: “find the simplest solution possible, and only increase complexity when needed.” The most successful implementations they observed used simple, composable patterns — direct LLM API calls, not frameworks — often implementable in a few lines of code.

item.title: History of Tool Use in Language Models
item.description: Schick et al. published “Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools” (Meta AI, February 2023, arXiv 2302.04761). They showed that a language model could learn, through self-supervised training, to decide when a function call would help and what arguments to pass. The model inserted API calls into its own training data, kept the ones that improved prediction, and fine-tuned on the result. This demonstrated that tool use could be learned rather than hand-coded.

item.title: Multi-Agent Failure Modes
item.description:
The MASFT study (arXiv 2503.13657, March 2025) analyzed 150+ execution traces across 5 multi-agent frameworks with 6 expert annotators (Cohen’s Kappa 0.88). The central finding: multi-agent architectures showed minimal performance gains over single-agent baselines on popular benchmarks. Some multi-agent systems performed worse than best-of-N sampling from a single agent — running the same single agent N times and taking the best result outperformed the coordinated multi-agent system.

Optimizing prompts and redesigning coordination topology produced only modest improvements: +5% for AG2 MathChat, +14% maximum for ChatDev with topology redesign.

item.title: Toward a Falsifiable Definition of Agentic Systems
item.description:
1. The problem

The term “agent” in AI discourse as of early 2026 refers to everything from a chatbot with web search to a multi-model system that writes, tests, and deploys code autonomously. As Bent (2025, arXiv 2508.05338) puts it: “The term ‘agent’ has been diluted beyond utility.” If the word means everything, it distinguishes nothing.

A falsifiable definition specifies observable conditions a system must meet. Its value is practical: it tells us what properties a system must demonstrate before we can predict its behavior, assess its risks, or compare it to alternatives.

item.title: Companion Tools for OpenClaw, March 2026
item.description:
Purpose

This text surveys the tools that most often recur around OpenClaw in current official documentation and practical guide material as of March 9, 2026. The source base does not support exact usage counts, so “often used” here means recurring companions in the current OpenClaw ecosystem rather than measured market share.

What the tool stack reveals

The current OpenClaw ecosystem is converging on a distinctive practice. OpenClaw itself stays as the long-running control plane, memory layer, and multi-channel agent surface. Specialized work is pushed outward:

item.title: OpenClaw Research and Math Assistant Patterns, March 2026
item.description:
Purpose

This text surveys how current expert material suggests using OpenClaw as a research assistant and then extends that evidence into a cautious math-assistant pattern. The research-assistant material is directly grounded in sources. The math-assistant section is an inference from those sources because the surveyed expert writing contains little OpenClaw-specific mathematics guidance.

The research-assistant pattern

The clearest expert playbook comes from Carl Vellotti’s research workflow guide. Its structure is simple:

item.title: OpenClaw Summary, March 2026
item.description:
Purpose

This text surveys OpenClaw as of March 9, 2026. OpenClaw is a self-hosted personal AI assistant platform organized around a persistent OpenClaw Gateway that exposes one assistant across the channels and devices a user already uses.

What OpenClaw is

The OpenClaw repository describes the project as a personal AI assistant that runs on the user’s own devices. The README’s central claim is that the Gateway is only the control plane. The product is the assistant itself: an always-available agent that can answer through messaging platforms, web chat, and companion apps.

item.title: Using OpenClaw on Windows, March 2026
item.description:
Purpose

This text collects practical advice for using OpenClaw on Windows as of March 9, 2026. The source base combines official OpenClaw documentation with two independent guides that contain operational advice rather than only product description.

Baseline: treat Windows as a WSL2 host

The official OpenClaw guidance is consistent: on Windows, run OpenClaw through WSL2, with Ubuntu recommended. The Windows page states that native Windows installs are untested and more problematic, while the CLI and Gateway are expected to run inside Linux.

item.title: Survey of MCP servers for knowledge repositories
item.description:
Purpose

This text surveys the Model Context Protocol (MCP) server ecosystem as of March 2026, focused on what’s useful for managing a knowledge repository like the emsemioverse: ~3000 markdown files with YAML frontmatter, a predicate graph, skills, and formal proof formalizations in Lean 4 and Agda.

Configuration

MCP servers for Claude Code are configured in three scopes:

Scope Location Shared?
Project .mcp.json (project root) Yes, version-controlled
Local ~/.claude.json (per-project) No, personal
User ~/.claude.json (global) No, all projects

CLI management: claude mcp add|list|remove|get.

item.title: Agent orchestration ecosystem survey (March 2026)
item.description:
This text surveys the agent orchestration ecosystem as of March 2026, assessed against the requirements of the agential semioverse tech stack: a Python service that loads skills from a library, exposes them via HTTP and MCP, and enforces a library/engine boundary where all admissible operators live in the library.

Frameworks surveyed

Claude Agent SDK (Anthropic)

The SDK that powers Claude Code. Provides a conversation loop, built-in file/shell tools, automatic context compaction, session persistence, subagent spawning, and MCP extensibility. As of early 2026 it includes “agent teams” (TeammateTool) for peer-to-peer messaging between agents, removing the parent-relay bottleneck of the subagent model. Locked to Claude models. MIT license (Python SDK).

item.title: Encoding disciplinary methods for AI agents
item.description: When an agent works in a discipline directory, it needs to know not just what to do but how to reason. The methods of a discipline — what counts as evidence, how claims are validated, what forms of argument are legitimate — are among the biggest shapers of how work proceeds. This text surveys how methods are represented in knowledge management systems and what the ASR can learn from existing practice.

item.title: Per-directory agent instructions: patterns and practices
item.description:
Agent instruction files — AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, copilot-instructions.md — tell AI agents how to behave when working in a repository. Most start as a single root file. As repositories grow, the question arises: should subdirectories carry their own instructions?

This text surveys how per-directory agent instructions work across the ecosystem, what the research says about their effectiveness, and how the patterns apply to knowledge repositories (as opposed to code repositories).

item.title: Survey: AI Agents for Formal Mathematics (External Landscape, 2026)
item.description:
AI Agents for Formal Mathematics: External Landscape Survey

Survey of external tools, research, and practices for LLM agents doing formal mathematics with proof assistants. Focused on what exists outside this repository that could improve agent mathematical capability here.

1. LLM-Based Mathematical Reasoning Agents

DeepSeek-Prover-V2 (DeepSeek, 2025)

Formal theorem proving in Lean 4. Key innovation: subgoal decomposition — the model writes an informal proof sketch first, decomposes into formal subgoals, then proves each subgoal. Trained with GRPO (not PPO) reinforcement learning. Achieved 88.9% on MiniF2F-test, 49 out of 658 problems on FormalMATH. Open-weight (7B and 671B MoE variants).
